digestive.title: Human Digestive System
keyQuestions: What is the role of each organ in digestion?
What is the difference between mechanical and chemical digestion?
Where are nutrients absorbed into the blood?
What happens to food that cannot be digested?
Why do we need to digest food?
keyIdeas: The human digestive system breaks down food into smaller, soluble substances that can be absorbed into the blood. The path is: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and anus.
Digestion breaks food into soluble nutrients that can be absorbed into the blood. Mechanical digestion is physical (chewing, churning); chemical digestion uses enzymes.
The small intestine has a huge surface area covered with villi, which is why most nutrient absorption happens there. The large intestine mainly absorbs water.

glossary.definition: The process of breaking down food into smaller molecules that can be absorbed into the blood and used by the body.
glossary.template: The process of breaking down ___ into ___ that can be ___.

glossary.definition: Physical breaking down of food into smaller pieces without changing its chemical nature. E.g. chewing by teeth, churning in the stomach.
Breaking down food using chemicals (enzymes and acids). Changes the chemical structure of food molecules into smaller ones that can be absorbed.
A biological chemical that speeds up the breakdown of food molecules. Different enzymes break down different nutrients (e.g. amylase breaks down starch).
The process by which digested nutrients pass through the wall of the small intestine into the bloodstream, where they are carried to cells.
Wave-like muscle contractions in the oesophagus and intestines that push food along the digestive tract.
Solid waste remaining after digestion and water absorption in the large intestine. Expelled through the anus.
Liquid produced by salivary glands in the mouth. Contains the enzyme amylase that begins breaking down starch, and lubricates food for swallowing.

respiratory.title: Respiratory System
keyQuestions: Why do our cells need oxygen?
What path does air take from the nose to the lungs?
What is the role of the alveoli in gas exchange?
What happens to the diaphragm when we inhale?
What happens to the ribs during exhalation?
How is exhaled air different from inhaled air?
What is diffusion and how does it apply to gas exchange?
Why do the alveoli have a large surface area?
How do cilia and mucus protect the lungs?
What effect does smoking have on the respiratory system?
keyIdeas: The human respiratory system takes in oxygen and releases carbon dioxide. Air travels through the nose, trachea, bronchi, and into the lungs, where oxygen passes into the blood and carbon dioxide leaves the blood.
Breathing is the movement of air into and out of the lungs. Respiration is the release of energy from food inside cells; the two are different.
The alveoli are the site of gas exchange. Their thin moist walls and rich blood supply allow oxygen to diffuse into the blood and carbon dioxide to diffuse out.

glossary.definition: The process by which cells use oxygen to release energy from food. Carbon dioxide and water are produced as waste products. Do not confuse with breathing — respiration happens inside cells.
glossary.template: The process by which living things release ___ from ___ by using ___.

glossary.definition: The windpipe. A tube reinforced with rings of cartilage that carries air from the throat down to the lungs. It splits into two bronchi at the bottom.
Two large tubes (singular: bronchus) that branch off the trachea, one leading into each lung. They further divide into smaller bronchioles.
Smaller branches of the bronchi inside the lungs. They carry air to and from the alveoli. Smooth muscle in their walls can dilate or constrict airflow.
Tiny air sacs (singular: alveolus) at the end of the bronchioles. Their thin, moist walls and rich blood supply make them the site of gas exchange between air and blood.
A dome-shaped muscle below the lungs. When it contracts (flattens), the chest volume increases and air is drawn in. When it relaxes (curves upward), air is pushed out.
Breathing in. The diaphragm contracts and ribs move up and out, increasing chest volume, decreasing pressure, so air flows into the lungs.
Breathing out. The diaphragm relaxes and ribs move down and in, decreasing chest volume, increasing pressure, so air is pushed out of the lungs.

The movement of particles from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration. Oxygen diffuses from the alveoli (high O₂) into the blood (low O₂). CO₂ diffuses in the opposite direction.
The process by which oxygen passes from the alveoli into the blood and carbon dioxide passes from the blood into the alveoli. Occurs at the alveolar walls by diffusion.
Tiny hair-like structures lining the airways. They beat rhythmically to sweep mucus (with trapped dust and bacteria) upward toward the throat, keeping the lungs clean.
A sticky substance produced by cells lining the airways. It traps dust, pollen, and bacteria before they can reach the lungs. Cilia move the mucus to be swallowed or coughed out.

circulatory.title: Circulatory System
keyQuestions: What are the components of blood and their functions?
What is the difference between arteries, veins, and capillaries?
How does blood flow through the heart?
Which is the ONLY artery that carries deoxygenated blood?
Why does the left ventricle have thicker walls than the right?
keyIdeas: The human circulatory system is a network of the heart, blood, and blood vessels. The heart pumps blood around the body, carrying oxygen and digested food to all cells and carrying waste away.
The heart has two sides. The right side pumps blood to the lungs to pick up oxygen; the left side pumps oxygenated blood to the rest of the body.

Arteries carry blood away from the heart (thick muscular walls). Veins carry blood back to the heart (thin walls, valves). Capillaries are one cell thick, where oxygen and nutrients pass into body cells.

glossary.definition: An upper chamber of the heart that receives blood. The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body; the left atrium receives oxygenated blood from the lungs.
A lower chamber of the heart that pumps blood out. The right ventricle pumps to the lungs; the left ventricle pumps to the entire body.
A blood vessel that carries blood AWAY from the heart. Has thick, elastic walls to withstand high pressure. Usually carries oxygenated blood — EXCEPT the pulmonary artery.
A blood vessel that carries blood TOWARDS the heart. Has thinner walls and valves to prevent backflow. Usually carries deoxygenated blood — EXCEPT the pulmonary veins.
The tiniest blood vessel — only one cell thick. Connects arteries to veins in organs and tissues. Site of exchange: O₂ and nutrients pass into cells; CO₂ and waste pass into blood.
A protein inside red blood cells that binds to oxygen. Gives blood its red colour. Carries O₂ from the lungs to body cells, and helps carry some CO₂ back.
Blood rich in oxygen. Appears bright red. Found in pulmonary veins, left side of heart, and arteries (except pulmonary artery).
Blood low in oxygen (but rich in CO₂). Appears dark red/purple. Found in veins (except pulmonary veins), right side of heart, and pulmonary artery.
The largest artery in the body. Carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle to the entire body. Branches into smaller arteries.
Carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs for oxygenation. The ONLY artery carrying deoxygenated blood — a common exam question!
Carries oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left atrium. The ONLY vein carrying oxygenated blood.
The largest vein. Carries deoxygenated blood from the body back to the right atrium of the heart.
The straw-coloured liquid part of blood (~55% of blood volume). Carries nutrients, hormones, CO₂, and waste products. Water makes up most of plasma.
The number of times the heart beats per minute (bpm). Average resting rate: 60–100 bpm. Can be felt as a pulse in arteries (e.g. wrist, neck). Increases during exercise.

circuits.title: Electrical System & Circuits
keyQuestions: What makes a circuit complete?
How does a series circuit differ from a parallel circuit?
Why does adding more bulbs in series make them dimmer?
What is the difference between a conductor and an insulator?
What happens when a switch is open?
keyIdeas: An electrical circuit is a complete path through which electric current can flow. A circuit must contain a source of electrical energy (battery or cell) and a conducting path, usually with a component such as a bulb.
A material is a conductor if it allows electricity (or heat) to pass through easily, and an insulator if it does not. Metals are good conductors; plastic, rubber, and wood are insulators.
A circuit is a complete path for electric current. If the path is broken (open switch, faulty wire), current cannot flow and no component will work.

Series circuits share a single loop: adding bulbs makes them dimmer, and one failed bulb stops them all. Parallel circuits have separate branches: each bulb gets full voltage, and a failure in one does not affect the others.

glossary.definition: A closed path through which electricity can flow. Must include a source of electricity, connecting wires, and at least one component.
glossary.template: A complete path through which ___ can flow.

glossary.definition: The flow of electric charge through a conductor. Measured in amperes (A). Flows from the positive to the negative terminal of the battery.
Components connected in a single loop. Current flows through each component one after another. If one bulb fails, all go out.
Components connected in separate branches. Each branch gets the full voltage. If one bulb fails, the others stay on.
A material that allows electricity to flow through it easily. E.g. copper, iron, aluminium, graphite.
glossary.template: A material that allows ___ to pass through ___.

glossary.definition: A material that does not allow electricity to flow through it. E.g. rubber, plastic, wood, glass, dry air.
glossary.template: A material that does not allow ___ to pass through ___.

glossary.definition: A device that opens or closes a circuit. Open switch = incomplete circuit = no current. Closed switch = complete circuit = current flows.
A source of electrical energy. A cell provides a voltage that drives current around the circuit. A battery is two or more cells joined together.
The "push" that drives current through a circuit. Measured in volts (V). More voltage → brighter bulbs (up to a point).
An instrument used to measure electric current. Always connected in series in a circuit.
The thin wire inside a bulb that heats up and glows when current passes through it.
The opposition to the flow of electric current in a circuit. Measured in ohms (Ω). Higher resistance = less current for the same voltage. Caused by collisions between electrons and atoms.
The relationship between voltage, current, and resistance: I = V ÷ R (Current = Voltage ÷ Resistance). Doubling the voltage doubles the current; doubling the resistance halves the current.

plants.title: Photosynthesis & Plants
keyQuestions: What are the raw materials for photosynthesis?
What are the products of photosynthesis?
What is the role of chlorophyll?
Where does photosynthesis take place in the plant cell?
How do plants transport water and food?
What is transpiration and why is it important?
keyIdeas: The plant transport system carries water and mineral salts from the roots through the stem (via the xylem) to the leaves. It also carries food made in the leaves to the rest of the plant.

Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make food using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide, producing food (in the form of starch) and releasing oxygen. It takes place in the leaves, in cells containing chlorophyll.
Plants have specialised cells and tissues. Leaves carry out photosynthesis; roots absorb water and mineral salts; the stem transports water up and food down.
Transpiration is the loss of water vapour from leaves through tiny pores called stomata. It helps pull water up the plant from the roots.

glossary.definition: The process by which green plants use light energy, carbon dioxide, and water to produce glucose and oxygen. Occurs in the chloroplasts.
glossary.template: The process by which green plants make ___ using ___, ___, and ___, producing ___.

glossary.definition: The green pigment in plant cells that absorbs light energy. Found inside chloroplasts. Gives leaves their green colour.
glossary.template: The green substance in ___ that absorbs ___ for ___.

glossary.definition: The organelle (structure) in plant cells where photosynthesis takes place. Contains chlorophyll.
A type of sugar produced during photosynthesis. Used by the plant as an energy source for growth, repair, and reproduction. Can be stored as starch.
Tiny pores (openings) on the underside of leaves. Allow carbon dioxide to enter and oxygen and water vapour to exit. Controlled by guard cells.
The process by which water is lost from a plant through the stomata as water vapour. Helps pull water up from roots through the xylem.
Tube-like vessels that transport water and dissolved minerals from roots to leaves. Part of the plant's vascular system.
Tube-like vessels that transport dissolved food (glucose/sucrose) from leaves to all other parts of the plant.
Tiny hair-like extensions of root cells that increase the surface area for absorbing water and minerals from the soil.
A gas in the air that is a raw material for photosynthesis. Enters the leaf through the stomata.
A stored form of glucose in plants. Tested for using iodine solution (turns blue-black if starch is present).
Pairs of cells surrounding each stoma. Open the stomata in light (for photosynthesis) and close them to prevent excess water loss.

cell-system.title: Cells & Body Systems
keyQuestions: What is a cell and why is it called the basic unit of life?
What are the main parts of a cell and their functions?
What are the key differences between plant cells and animal cells?
What is the hierarchy from cell to organism?
Name three organ systems and their main functions.
What is a specialised cell? Give two examples.
keyIdeas: A system is made up of parts that work together to perform a function. If a part is missing or broken, the system cannot work properly.

Human systems work together: the digestive system absorbs nutrients, the respiratory system supplies oxygen, the circulatory system transports both to every cell so that the cells can respire and release energy.
Cells are the basic units of life. Similar cells form a tissue; tissues form an organ; organs form an organ system; organ systems form a whole organism.

glossary.definition: The basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms. Every living thing is made of one or more cells.
A thin, flexible layer surrounding every cell. Controls what enters and exits the cell. Present in both plant and animal cells.
A rigid outer layer found ONLY in plant cells (and fungi). Made of cellulose. Provides shape and support. Not present in animal cells.
The control centre of the cell. Contains DNA (genetic instructions). Controls cell activities and reproduction.
A jelly-like fluid filling the cell. Where most chemical reactions take place. Contains the organelles.
Found ONLY in plant cells (in green parts). Contains chlorophyll. Site of photosynthesis. Gives plants their green colour.
A fluid-filled sac. Plant cells have a large central vacuole (stores water, keeps cell firm/turgid). Animal cells have small, temporary vacuoles.
A group of similar cells working together to perform a specific function. e.g. muscle tissue, nerve tissue, leaf tissue.
A structure made of different tissues working together for a specific function. e.g. heart, lung, stomach, leaf.
A group of organs working together to perform a major body function. e.g. digestive system, respiratory system.
A complete, individual living thing. Made up of organ systems working together.

A cell with a specific shape/structure that suits its particular function. e.g. red blood cell (flat, biconcave — carries oxygen), nerve cell (long — transmits signals), root hair cell (elongated — absorbs water).

questions.stem: Explain why a plant needs both water and sunlight to survive.
questions.modelAnswer: A plant needs water and sunlight for photosynthesis, the process by which it makes food. Sunlight provides the energy, which is absorbed by chlorophyll. Water, together with carbon dioxide, is a raw material that is converted into food (starch). Without either water or sunlight, the plant cannot make food and will eventually die.

questions.stem: A green plant was placed in a dark cupboard for two weeks. Describe and explain what would happen to the plant.
questions.modelAnswer: The plant would turn yellow and eventually die. In the dark, the plant cannot receive sunlight, so photosynthesis cannot take place. The plant is unable to make food. Once the food stored in the plant is used up, the plant has no energy to carry out life processes and dies.

questions.stem: Alexey noticed that his heart beats faster after he runs. Explain why.
questions.modelAnswer: When Alexey runs, his muscles work harder and need more energy. To release more energy, the muscle cells need more oxygen and digested food. The heart beats faster to pump more blood around the body, so that oxygen and digested food are delivered to the muscle cells more quickly, and waste carbon dioxide is removed more quickly.

questions.stem: Explain how the digestive system and the circulatory system work together to keep the body alive.
questions.modelAnswer: The digestive system breaks down food into small, soluble substances in the small intestine. These digested substances are absorbed into the blood. The circulatory system then transports the digested food in the blood to all the cells of the body. The cells use the digested food, together with oxygen, to release energy for life processes.

questions.stem: In a circuit with a cell, a wire, a bulb, and a switch, the bulb does not light up when the switch is closed. Suggest two possible reasons.
questions.modelAnswer: First, the circuit may not be complete: a wire could be loose or broken, so current cannot flow through the bulb. Second, the cell may be flat, meaning it no longer provides electrical energy to the circuit. A third possibility is that the bulb is blown, so current cannot pass through its filament.

human-reproduction.title: Human Reproduction
keyQuestions: What is the function of the reproductive organs in males and females?
What is fertilisation?
How does a baby develop before birth?
What changes occur during puberty?
keyIdeas: Human reproduction is sexual: a male gamete (sperm) and a female gamete (egg) fuse during fertilisation to form a zygote.
The zygote develops into a foetus inside the mother's uterus. The foetus receives oxygen and nutrients from the mother via the placenta and umbilical cord.
Gestation in humans lasts about 9 months. After birth, the baby depends on adults for food, warmth, and protection for many years.
Puberty brings physical and emotional changes, triggered by hormones. Girls typically reach puberty earlier than boys.

glossary.definition: The process by which living things produce offspring to continue their species.
Female organ that produces eggs (ova). Females have two ovaries.
The organ in which the fertilised egg develops into a baby.
Male organs that produce sperm. Males have two testes.
The joining of a sperm cell with an egg cell (ovum) to form a fertilised egg (zygote).
The early stage of development after fertilisation (first 8 weeks).
The developing baby from 8 weeks until birth.
The period of physical changes during which children develop into adults capable of reproduction.
The monthly shedding of the uterine lining when no fertilisation occurs.
